draft.introduction: Revelation 13:1–18 describes the two beasts—one from the sea, one from the earth—and introduces the number 666. The imagery encodes a critique of imperial Rome’s claims to divine authority. From Codex Sinaiticus.

translation.text: And I saw a beast rising out of the sea, with ten horns and seven heads, with ten diadems on its horns and blasphemous names on its heads. And the whole earth marvelled as they followed the beast. And they worshiped the dragon, for he had given his authority to the beast, and they worshiped the beast, saying, “Who is like the beast, and who can fight against it?”
translation.original_text: και ειδον εκ της θαλασσης θηριον αναβαινον

translation.text: Also it causes all, both small and great, both rich and poor, both free and slave, to be marked on the right hand or the forehead, so that no one can buy or sell unless he has the mark, that is, the name of the beast or the number of its name. This calls for wisdom: let the one who has understanding calculate the number of the beast, for it is the number of a man, and his number is 666.
translation.original_text: και ποιει παντας τους μικρους και τους μεγαλους

translator_notes.note: The number 666 (χξς in Greek numerals) most likely encodes “Nero Caesar” in Hebrew gematria (נרון קסר = 50+200+6+50+100+60+200 = 666). Sinaiticus reads 666; a few manuscripts read 616, which corresponds to the Latin form “Nero” without the final nun.
